text: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: We spent most of today's sync talking about the code-switching problem that keeps showing up in the Urdu ASR logs. Basically what's happening is a lot of our users will start a sentence in Urdu and then drop into English mid-utterance, usually for technical terms or brand names, and the model just isn't handling that transition well. Sometimes it tries to force an Urdu phonetic mapping onto the English word and the output is unintelligible, other times it seems to just drop the English segment

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: times it seems to just drop the English segment entirely and the transcript has a gap in it.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: Someone brought up that this is a pretty well known problem in multilingual ASR generally, not just something specific to our pipeline, but the severity does seem to vary a lot depending on how much code-switched data was in the training set to begin with. Our current training data is almost entirely monolingual Urdu, which is probably why this is hitting us harder than it might hit a team that trained on more naturalistic conversational data from the start.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: There was some debate about whether to fix this at the tokenizer level or push it further upstream into a dedicated language identification pass before the main ASR model even sees the audio. The tokenizer approach is faster to prototype since we already have the script-boundary detection code from the text normalization work, but a few people pointed out that audio-level cues like accent and prosody shift might actually be more reliable signals for detecting a code-switch than anything we can

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: for detecting a code-switch than anything we can pull from partial transcription output alone, since by the time you have a partial transcript to analyze the damage from a bad phonetic mapping may already be done.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: We also touched on Pashto and Punjabi briefly. Pashto has less code-switching in the data we've seen so far, at least in the samples reviewed this week, but that might just be a reflection of the current data sources rather than a real difference in how speakers actually talk. Punjabi is trickier because there's the whole Gurmukhi versus Shahmukhi script question layered on top of the code-switching question, and nobody in the room felt confident making a call on priority there without pulling

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: making a call on priority there without pulling more data first.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: Action items coming out of the code-switching discussion: someone is going to pull a sample of one hundred code-switched utterances from the existing Urdu logs so we have a concrete error analysis to look at before the next sync, rather than just going off general impressions. There was also a suggestion to check whether any of the open multilingual speech corpora have annotated code-switch boundaries we could borrow for a quick baseline, since building our own annotated set from scratch would

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: building our own annotated set from scratch would take a while and we don't want to block the tokenizer prototype on that.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: Moving on, we spent maybe fifteen minutes on the Pashto dialect coverage issue that's been flagged a few times now. The gist of it is that most of our Pashto training data comes from a small number of broadcast archive sources, and those sources skew heavily toward a couple of regional dialects. We're seeing higher error rates in informal user feedback from speakers in other regions, but nobody's actually quantified this properly yet, it's all anecdotal so far from support tickets.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: The proposal on the table is to run a targeted crowd-sourcing campaign specifically aimed at underrepresented dialect regions rather than just collecting more Pashto data generally, since general collection tends to skew toward whoever is easiest to reach, which is usually the same overrepresented regions we already have plenty of data from. This would need coordination with whoever handles the crowd-sourcing platform's regional targeting settings, which apparently is possible but hasn't been

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: which apparently is possible but hasn't been used by our team before, so there's a bit of a learning curve there.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: There was a side discussion about whether we should be tracking dialect as an explicit metadata field going forward, similar to how we already tag script variant for Punjabi. General feeling in the room was yes, this is overdue, but it needs someone to actually define what the dialect taxonomy should look like before it can be added to the ingestion schema, and that's not a small task since dialect boundaries aren't always clean geographic lines.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: Third item was an update on the shared front-end architecture question. As a reminder, the front end is trained jointly across all three languages, and the hypothesis has always been that shared acoustic features transfer well even across languages with different phonology. This has held up reasonably well for Pashto benefiting from the larger Urdu dataset, but the tonal distinction issue in Punjabi that's been discussed before doesn't seem to be resolved just by adding more Punjabi data.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: Someone raised the possibility of adding a language-specific adapter layer right after the shared front end, before the branching point, specifically to recover tonal information that might be getting flattened out in the shared representation. This is a bigger architectural change than anything else on the roadmap right now so it would need a proper design review before anyone starts implementing, but there was general agreement it's worth prototyping given how long the tonal gap has persisted

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: given how long the tonal gap has persisted despite other fixes.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: We briefly discussed the Q2 latency incident again, mostly because a very similar change is being proposed now and people wanted to make sure we don't repeat the same mistake. Last time, updating the shared front end increased inference time enough to push Urdu latency over target under peak load, and it wasn't caught until it was already affecting a meaningful chunk of production traffic. This time around there was a suggestion to run the updated front end through a proper load test on the

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: front end through a proper load test on the actual reference serving configuration before it goes anywhere near a shadow deployment, rather than just checking accuracy metrics in an offline evaluation and assuming latency will be fine.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: Fourth item was a shorter update on Sindhi. Early data collection is still in progress, nothing major to report, but there was a question about whether Sindhi should share the existing front end or get its own from the start given the tonal transfer issues we're already seeing with Punjabi. No decision made, this is going to depend partly on how the Punjabi adapter layer prototype goes, since if that works well it probably makes sense to just build Sindhi support on the same pattern rather than

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: Sindhi support on the same pattern rather than inventing something new.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: Fifth and last item was a quick check-in on the drift detection system. It's been running for about two months now and has fired three alerts, two of which were real regressions caught early and one which turned out to be a false positive related to a temporary spike in noisy audio from a single crowd-sourcing batch rather than an actual model quality issue. General consensus is the false positive rate is acceptable given what it caught, but there was a suggestion to add a quick automated check

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: was a suggestion to add a quick automated check for anomalous batch composition before the drift alert fires, so that the two failure modes, real model drift versus a weird data batch, are easier to tell apart without someone manually digging into it every time.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda

text: No further items were raised. Next sync will focus mostly on reviewing the hundred-utterance code-switch error analysis once it's ready, plus whatever comes out of the crowd-sourcing platform's regional targeting research for the Pashto campaign. Meeting ran about fifty five minutes, longer than usual, mostly because of the tonal adapter layer discussion which ended up being more involved than expected.

section_headings: Meeting notes - speech team sync - full agenda
